And like the political thing, yeah, there was a time when the profile of our customer was pretty narrow and very clear because of source. And so generally speaking, farm or business owners, small business owners, and entrepreneurs of relatively small companies are conservative, more than they are liberal, and would identify themselves as Republican or independent before they would identify themselves as Democrats. And so my best guess about where we are now is probably in the 50-50 range as opposed to the 90-10 range at some point. Some of that is also youth. You know, the average age of our customer has come down because of the influence of the Internet as opposed to where it was before. And you know, there was a period of years, say seven of them, where probably 75% of the total base of new customers was coming from Peter Low events. Well, you're pretty much, except for the Tony events, the four ill-fated Tony events, I mean, you're pretty much, that's a very conservative, very politic, to the point that occasionally when, because of his nonprofit status, when he would put like a liberal political figure on, people boo. I mean, Mario Cuomo comes out and everybody's booing. Time he's done, I'm booing because the smucks, you know, 35 minutes over and people are streaming out in droves, you know. So, you know, it was pretty clear what we had, you know. Now it's a lot fuzzier. However, I still go there and here's why, because they're the more valuable customers. So, I'll still err on the side of connecting with the ones who I know and believe have the greatest value to us over time. I guess what I'm saying though is I can see if you were in marketing for somehow things that you shipped and you got yourself, you read the newsletter now, just like 90% of the people love... Well, let's say they're all liberals. Yeah, you kind of... I would... Now you now avoid, I guess what I'm saying is that in my case, I'm the Republican... I would mostly avoid it and I would really work at finding opportunities to connect with it where I could, right? And that's not impossible for me. I mean, I'm a fiscal conservative but I'm kind of moderate socially. So, I can find common ground. It just wouldn't be as much common ground. But yeah, when you can't at all, then the best you can do is ignore and look for the other point of connections, right? Page 125, Principle 18. This is called the Principle of Multiplicity. You guys will like this one. And this says that different people have different reasons for buying into an idea, proposition, opportunity, etc. And, you know, we basically have two choices, right? We can figure out how to segment them. So, like the problem with the profiles that we all just built is there an oversimplification of our customer base, right? So, some people value family more than prominence to go back to yours, Michael. Others would value prominence. So, you got all the right items, probably, but I would put them in a different priority than he would put them in a different priority than he would put them in a different priority, right? So, our choices are when we can, we segment somehow or let them segment themselves and we change what we talk to them about based on the little micro segments that we can get them into or that they put themselves into. So, for example, simply separating yours into married or single, now against that profile, right, helps us if there were enough of them and if that justified the effort in order to talk to them differently. The other thing, of course, is to make sure that we touch every base, every reason why they might, putting it in the best priority order that we can for what we believe to be the majority of what we've got. But part of the trick is making sure we have figured out what every base is, right, every possible reason. And there's the connection of the reason to the value, right? And so, what I've shown you here is, so this is Melvin Powers' old list for why somebody might want to write and publish a book, right? And he had 10 main reasons that they might want to do that, that he tried to build into all his ads and all the sales letters selling his stuff about how you could write and publish books, right? Then I've, in boldface and parentheses, I've identified what I believe to be the value behind that reason, which the emotional connection, the thing that they're really after by being motivated by that reason. And so, the principle of multiplicity says you want to try and identify the biggest, most complete list that you can of significant motivators to the people you wish to influence and identify not just the reason, but also the value behind the reason that is really the strongest motivator. Having, if you build this, of course, you have, again, a resource you can use, continue to update it and improve it, but you have a checklist that really ought to be checked every time you write to them, be it book, newsletter, fax, sales letter, whatever, how many of these bases are we hitting? Or are we segmenting them and hitting different bases with different ones?
